title: Owning Your Word and Your Work

required.title: [Read Aloud] Salt in His Shoes
required.author: Deloris Jordan and Roslyn M. Jordan

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] The Berenstain Bears and the Truth
suggested.author: Stan and Jan Berenstain

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] A Chair for My Mother
suggested.author: Vera B. Williams

competence_markers.description: Does the child follow through on commitments consistently?
Do they own mistakes immediately or attempt to hide or deflect?
Do they take initiative without prompting?
Do they accept correction without defensiveness?
Do they manage their own belongings, space, and preparation?
Is there visible growth in these areas over weeks and months?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you said you would do something and you followed through, even when it was hard or boring.
narration_prompts.detail: What made it hard? What made you do it anyway?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you made a mistake and owned it right away.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? How did it feel? What happened after you told the truth?
Can you give me an example of a time you saw something that needed doing and did it without being asked?

narration_prompts.prompt: When someone corrects you or tells you to do something better, what is your first reaction?
narration_prompts.detail: What should it be? Why is it hard to accept correction?

narration_prompts.prompt: What are the things you are responsible for every day — your stuff, your space, your preparation?
narration_prompts.detail: How well are you managing them? What would it look like if you managed them perfectly?

cross_connections.description: The daily habit structure that makes self-leadership concrete and visible
The follow-through and completion discipline that self-leadership requires
The emotional steadiness needed to own mistakes and accept correction without falling apart
Physical training as the earliest and most tangible practice of personal discipline and self-leadership
Self-leadership requires making choices and living with consequences — the foundation of decision training
A person who leads themselves well is ready to begin working effectively with others

content.why_this_matters:
Before you can ever lead anyone else, you have to be able to lead yourself. That sounds like something grown-ups say that does not mean very much, but it means something very specific: Can you do what you said you would do? Can you finish what you started? Can you tell the truth when you made a mistake, even when it is uncomfortable?

These are not small things. They are the foundation of everything that comes later in leadership. A person who cannot keep their own promises will never be trusted to lead a team. A person who hides their mistakes will never be able to fix them. A person who waits to be told what to do when they can see what needs doing is not ready to be in charge of anything — including themselves.

This packet is about building the habits that make you someone other people can count on. Not because someone is watching. Not because you will get in trouble. Because you decided that your word means something, and your work reflects who you are.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Follow-through on commitments** — the habit of completing what you promised, fully and on time
2. **Ownership of mistakes** — the practice of admitting errors immediately and honestly
3. **Initiative without prompting** — seeing what needs to be done and doing it without waiting for instruction
4. **Accepting correction without deflection** — responding to feedback with "I will fix it" rather than excuses
5. **Personal preparation and organization** — managing your own belongings, arriving ready, keeping your space ordered
6. **Reliability as a character trait** — becoming someone whose word others trust because they have watched you keep it
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Word Is You

When you tell someone you are going to do something — bring the supplies, finish the drawing, help clean up, be ready by a certain time — you have made a promise. It does not matter whether you said "I promise" in a formal way. If you said you would do it, your word is on the line.

Why does this matter so much? Because the way people decide whether to trust you is by watching what you do after you say you will do something. If you say "I will bring the markers" and you bring the markers, people learn that your word means something. If you say "I will bring the markers" and you forget, and then the next time you say "I will clean up my area" and you do not, people learn something different. They learn that what you say and what you do are not the same thing.

This is not about being perfect. Everyone forgets sometimes. Everyone makes mistakes. The difference is what you do about it. The person who forgets the markers and says, "I forgot — I am sorry, and I will bring them tomorrow, and I will make sure I do not forget again" is still someone whose word matters to them. The person who forgets and says, "It is not a big deal" or "someone else should have brought them" is someone who does not take their own word seriously.

Here is something important: nobody starts out being perfectly reliable. It is a skill you build, like any other skill. You build it by practicing. Every time you say you will do something and then do it, you are training yourself. Every time you follow through when it is inconvenient or boring or harder than you expected, you are getting stronger. And every time you let it slide — every time you give yourself an excuse for not doing what you said — you are training yourself in the wrong direction.

### Part 2: Owning Your Mistakes

Everybody makes mistakes. You will spill things, break things, forget things, say things you should not have said, and do things you knew you were not supposed to do. This is normal. It is part of being human and part of being young.

What matters is not whether you make mistakes. What matters is what you do in the first ten seconds after you realize you made one.

There are two paths. The first path is the hard one: you go to the person who needs to know, and you say what happened. "I broke the cup." "I forgot to do my task." "I said something mean to her and I should not have." This path is uncomfortable. Your stomach might feel tight. You might worry about getting in trouble. But this path is the one that builds something inside you that nothing else can build — the knowledge that you can face the truth about yourself and say it out loud.

The second path is the easy one in the moment but the hard one in the long run: you hide it, you blame someone else, you wait to be caught, or you pretend it did not happen. This path feels easier right away. But it costs something. It costs your honesty with yourself. And if someone else gets blamed for what you did, it costs their trust in you — sometimes permanently.

Here is something adults rarely tell children: the reason we want you to own your mistakes is not mainly about the mistake. Broken cups can be replaced. Forgotten tasks can be redone. The reason it matters is that a person who can say "I did this and it was wrong" is a person who can be trusted with more responsibility. A person who hides mistakes is a person who cannot be given responsibility, because the people around them will never know when something has gone wrong until it is too late to fix it.

The habit starts now, with small things. When you practice telling the truth about a broken cup at age six, you are training yourself to tell the truth about a bigger mistake at age sixteen. The muscle is the same. The only difference is the weight.

### Part 3: Taking Initiative — Seeing What Needs Doing

Initiative means doing something that needs to be done without being told to do it. It is one of the earliest and most visible signs that a person is ready for responsibility.

You walk into a room and see that the supplies are scattered everywhere. Nobody has asked you to clean them up. What do you do? If you wait for someone to tell you, you are being obedient — but you are not leading. If you start picking things up because you can see they need to be picked up, that is initiative. You saw a problem and you acted on it.

Initiative does not mean doing whatever you want. It means looking around and asking yourself: "What needs to be done right now that I could do?" Sometimes the answer is obvious — the floor is messy, a younger child needs help carrying something, the materials for the next activity are not set up yet. Sometimes it is less obvious — you notice that someone seems upset and you check on them, or you realize the plan for the day has a problem and you mention it before it becomes a bigger problem.

Why does this matter for leadership? Because a leader is someone who sees what needs to happen and makes it happen. If you cannot do this for yourself — in a room, with small tasks, at your age — you will not be able to do it for a team when you are older. Initiative is a habit. Like follow-through, it gets stronger every time you practice it and weaker every time you ignore what you see.

Start small. When you see something that needs doing, do it. Do not announce it. Do not ask for praise. Just do it because it needed doing and you were there. Over time, people will notice. And more importantly, you will notice — you will feel the difference between being someone who waits and being someone who acts.

### Part 4: Accepting Correction

When someone tells you that your work is not done well enough, or that you made an error, or that you need to do something differently, your first reaction matters.

Most people's first reaction to correction is to defend themselves. "It is not my fault." "I did do it right." "That is not fair." This reaction is natural. Nobody likes being told they fell short. But this reaction, if you let it become a habit, will stop you from learning anything from the correction.

The practice is simple and difficult: when someone corrects you, listen. Really listen — not the kind of listening where you are already planning what you will say back, but the kind where you are trying to understand what they are telling you. Then respond. If they are right, say so: "You are right. I will fix it." If you do not understand, ask: "Can you show me what you mean?" If you genuinely believe the correction is wrong, you can say so respectfully — but only after you have honestly considered whether they might be right.

Here is why this matters for self-leadership: a person who cannot accept correction cannot improve. They are stuck at whatever level they are at right now, because every piece of information that could help them get better bounces off their defensiveness. A person who accepts correction — who says "thank you for telling me" and means it — is a person who gets better every day. Over months and years, the gap between these two people becomes enormous.

The hardest corrections to accept are the ones that are true. When someone tells you something about yourself that you already suspected but did not want to hear — that you have been cutting corners, that you have not been trying your hardest, that you let someone else do the hard part — the temptation to get defensive is strongest exactly when the correction matters most.

### Part 5: Your Space, Your Preparation, Your Readiness

Self-leadership shows up in the most ordinary, unglamorous ways. Can you pack your own bag with everything you need? Can you keep your workspace organized so you can find things when you need them? Can you arrive prepared for the day's activities without someone else making sure you have everything?

These seem like small things. They are small things. But they are the small things that make all the bigger things possible. A person who shows up without their materials and says "I forgot" is a person who could not manage the simplest domain of responsibility — their own stuff. If you cannot manage your own stuff, why would anyone trust you to manage a project, a team, or a mission?

Start with what is yours. Your space, your belongings, your preparation. Make it your standard — not because someone checks, but because it is your standard. When you pack your bag the night before and you have everything you need the next morning, that is you leading yourself. When your workspace is organized because you organized it — not because someone told you to clean it — that is you leading yourself.

This connects to something bigger: the difference between doing things because someone is watching and doing things because they are your standard. At first, adults help you remember, remind you, check your work. That is fine — that is how learning works. But the goal is to get to the point where you do it because it is yours. Your standard. Your responsibility. Not because of the reminder, but because of who you are becoming.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Follow-Through on Commitments
**Developing well:** The child consistently completes tasks they have committed to, on time and to the stated standard. They do not need repeated reminders. When they realize they cannot meet a commitment, they communicate proactively rather than simply not showing up.

**Developing slowly:** The child frequently forgets commitments, requires multiple reminders, or completes tasks partially. Promises are made easily but not backed by action.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the commitments age-appropriate in scope? Is the child overcommitting (saying yes to everything without the capacity to follow through)? Are there visual reminders or systems in place to support developing memory and planning skills?

### 2. Ownership of Mistakes
**Developing well:** When the child makes a mistake, they disclose it promptly without being caught first. They can say what went wrong and what they will do differently. The gap between the mistake and the disclosure is shrinking over time.

**Developing slowly:** The child hides mistakes, blames others, or waits to be caught. When confronted, the first response is defensive or evasive. There is a pattern of "it wasn't me" or "it's not fair."

**Diagnostic questions:** How do adults in the child's life respond to disclosed mistakes? If confession is met with the same punishment as being caught, the child has no incentive to disclose. The response to honest ownership must be visibly different from the response to concealment — not in the consequence, but in the respect shown.

### 3. Initiative Without Prompting
**Developing well:** The child regularly notices things that need doing and acts without instruction. They help without being asked. They prepare for upcoming activities without reminders. Other children and adults comment on their helpfulness or awareness.

**Developing slowly:** The child waits for instructions on everything. They see problems (messes, younger children struggling, materials not ready) and do not act unless directed. Initiative exists only when the task is fun or when praise is expected.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child given genuine autonomy to act? If every action requires permission, initiative is structurally impossible. Are there domains where the child has both the authority and the expectation to act independently?

### 4. Accepting Correction Without Deflection
**Developing well:** The child listens to correction, considers it, and responds constructively — fixing the problem, asking for clarification, or acknowledging the issue. Defensive reactions are brief and followed by genuine engagement with the feedback.

**Developing slowly:** Correction consistently triggers defensiveness, tears, shutdown, or counterattack. The child cannot hear feedback without experiencing it as a personal attack. Growth in response to correction is minimal because the correction is not being processed.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is correction being delivered with specificity and respect? "You did this wrong" triggers defense. "The shelf is not level — can you see why? How would you fix it?" invites engagement. Is the ratio of correction to affirmation appropriate? A child who only hears what they did wrong will eventually stop listening.

### 5. Personal Organization and Readiness
**Developing well:** The child manages their belongings, packs their own materials, arrives prepared, and maintains their space to a reasonable standard. Systems are in place (a checklist, a hook for the bag, a routine for preparation) and the child uses them independently.

**Developing slowly:** The child is consistently unprepared, disorganized, or reliant on adults to manage their belongings and readiness. Materials are lost, spaces are chaotic, and preparation happens only under direct supervision.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the child have systems and physical infrastructure that support organization? (A place for everything, a checklist, a preparation routine.) Has the child been taught how to organize, or is organization assumed? Some children need explicit instruction in the mechanics before the habit can form.
